blurb: Alphabet's free cash flow turned negative last quarter as its capex forecast climbed into territory that spooked investors, even as cloud revenue kept accelerating. The market's read is that the AI buildout is now outrunning the returns it can point to, and Google is asking shareholders to fund a bet whose payback it can't yet quantify. When even the strongest cloud numbers get overshadowed by the spend line, the market is telling every hyperscaler that the tolerance for uncapped AI capex is thinning.

title: House Lawmakers Introduce Bipartisan AI ‘Kill Switch’ Bill Following OpenAI Cyber Incident
blurb: House lawmakers introduced a bipartisan bill that would require developers of the most advanced AI systems to keep the ability to slow, suspend, or shut down their models when they pose serious risks. The trigger was a recent OpenAI cyber incident, and bipartisan sponsorship means it has a real path forward rather than dying in committee. For founders building on frontier models, mandated kill-switch controls would push compliance, logging, and shutdown tooling into the roadmap, and the smaller labs you rely on will carry that cost too.

title: UK fintech funding cools to £1.1bn in H1 2026
blurb: UK fintech raised $1.5B in H1 2026, down 35% from the prior half and 26% year on year, according to Tracxn. The drop is almost entirely a late-stage story, with those rounds falling 45% to $830M while seed jumped 93% and early-stage stayed 27% above H1 2025. The signal for founders is caution at the scale-up stage, not a retreat from the sector, and megarounds held steady with six checks of $100M or more, including Paymentology's $175M Series A.

title: Travis Kalanick’s robotics company raises $1.7B, led by a16z
blurb: Travis Kalanick's robotics company Atoms raised $1.7B led by a16z, with Ben Horowitz taking a board seat and Uber, the company that ousted him in 2017, joining the round. Atoms is the rebranded holding company atop his CloudKitchens work, now folding in Anthony Levandowski's heavy-industry automation startup Pronto and aiming at a "wheelbase for robots" across mining and manufacturing. The check size signals that investors are backing physical-world AI at frontier-software valuations, betting the next Uber-scale outcome comes from automating heavy industry rather than another app.

title: OpenAI unveils Presence, a new platform that lets enterprises launch and manage realtime voice agents and chatbots
blurb: OpenAI announced Presence, an enterprise platform for deploying and managing realtime voice agents and chatbots that answer questions, access company systems, take approved actions, and escalate to humans under company-defined policies. It bundles the guardrails, system connections, evaluations, and update processes that founders usually have to stitch together themselves. It ships through a limited GA with OpenAI deployment engineers, is not self-service, and runs OpenAI models at the core while letting customers connect third-party models for guardrails and tools.

title: AI chip startup Etched defies skeptics, hits $10.3B valuation from big-name investors
blurb: Etched raised $300M at a $10.3B valuation led by Sequoia, doubling its $5B mark from seven months ago in what the company calls the highest valuation ever for a Sequoia-led Series C. The startup, founded by three Harvard dropouts to build inference-specific chips sold as full systems, has now manufactured its homegrown silicon, put first systems in clients' hands, and booked $1B in orders. A bet once dismissed as wacky, betting on transformer-optimized hardware, is now one Google is reportedly chasing with its own Frozen v2 chip for Gemini.

title: OpenAI’s early backer Khosla Ventures targets largest fund ever at $5.5B with most going to early-stage AI startups
blurb: Khosla Ventures is raising up to $5.5B across its newest funds, the largest haul in its 20-year history and close to 40% above its last vintage. More than half, $3B, is aimed at seed and early-stage AI startups, the riskiest stage and the one where the firm made its name writing OpenAI's first outside check. The bet doubles down on early AI at a moment when most capital is chasing later, safer rounds, and Khosla's recent deals in legal AI, defense, and robotics show where that money is headed.

title: Alphabet's cash burn raises alarm for Big Tech as AI spending climbs
blurb: Alphabet is burning through cash faster than it generates it as capex on AI data centers and chips outruns its operating profit. The concern spreading across Big Tech is that the hyperscalers are all committing to the same multi-year buildout at once, with returns still years out and depreciation about to hit their margins. For founders, the read is that the labs and clouds you depend on are now spending against a bet that has to pay off, which shapes how they price, bundle, and compete down the stack.

title: Insurance startup Corgi reportedly raised more money at $4B — its third round in 8 weeks
blurb: Corgi has reportedly closed a second Series B extension at a $4B valuation, its third round in eight weeks, doubling the mark it set in late May. The insurance startup raised its Series A in January, hit a $1.3B valuation in early May, then a $2.6B B1 three weeks later, and is now here. Backers point to revenue: the company was at a $40M run rate seven months ago and is reportedly tracking to $450M by year-end. Even in a funding frenzy full of back-to-back rounds, that cadence is the outlier.

title: Sources: AI robotics startup Genesis AI is in talks to raise around $500M in a new funding round at a $3B pre-money valuation (Bloomberg)
blurb: Genesis AI is in talks to raise around $500M at a $3B pre-money valuation, per Bloomberg, one of the largest rounds yet for an AI robotics startup still pre-revenue. The number signals that capital is rotating from pure software models toward the harder problem of putting AI into the physical world, where training data is scarcer and the feedback loops are slower. Investors are underwriting years of foundation-model work on the bet that robotics is where the next durable moats get built.

title: Tech Bonds Hit by Selloff as AI Debt Fears Race Through Markets
blurb: Investment-grade bonds from the largest US tech companies sold off Thursday as investors reassessed how much debt is funding the AI buildout, with Middle East tensions adding to the pressure. The concern is concentration: a handful of hyperscalers are borrowing heavily to fund compute capex on the bet that AI demand shows up on schedule, and any doubt about that timeline now moves credit markets, not just equities. For founders raising against AI infrastructure or selling into it, the cost of capital funding your customers just got more expensive and more scrutinized.

title: Google Cloud CEO Kurian says customers are spending 50% more as segment blows away expectations
blurb: Google Cloud grew 82% year-on-year last quarter, and CEO Thomas Kurian says existing customers now spend roughly 50% more than they committed to, driven by product differentiation and go-to-market execution. Demand is running so far ahead of capacity that Google will rent from neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius for a few quarters, taking a margin hit to capture customers who compound their spend over time. The catch is capex: Alphabet raised its 2026 forecast to as much as $205B and shares fell 7.1% as investors weighed the ballooning AI infrastructure bill.

title: Four former DOGE staffers raised $160 million at a $1.4 billion valuation for an AI military cyber startup
blurb: Four former DOGE staffers raised $160M at a $1.4B valuation for Cathedral, a stealth military cyber startup, with Andreessen Horowitz and Sequoia leading and taking board seats. The valuation reflects investor confidence in the team's government relationships rather than any product, since the company has no disclosed revenue or contracts and plans to sell AI-driven cyber operations back to agencies its founders recently ran. DOGE alumni are now converting government access into venture capital, entering a defense-tech market where Anduril just raised $5B at a $61B valuation.

title: Intel Benefits From a New Shift in A.I. Spending
blurb: Intel's revenue rose 25% last quarter, its fastest growth in 15 years, as AI companies increasingly bought the CPUs Intel builds rather than only the GPUs that dominated the buildout so far. The shift matters because AI infrastructure spending is broadening beyond Nvidia's accelerators into the general-purpose compute that surrounds them. For a chipmaker written off as a casualty of the AI era, the quarter is evidence that the boom is lifting more of the stack than the narrow set of winners everyone already priced in.

title: NVIDIA challenger AMD commits $5B to Anthropic and wins Microsoft in the same week
blurb: AMD landed two of the most demanding compute buyers in AI within a single week, its most serious challenge yet to Nvidia's grip on frontier infrastructure. Anthropic will deploy up to two gigawatts of AMD's Helios systems, backed by a capped investment of up to $5B released against deployment milestones rather than paid upfront, days after Microsoft committed Azure's AI workloads to the same platform. Both wins landed before Helios reaches general availability in late 2026, giving AMD two marquee validators while the chip is still unreleased.

title: Nvidia rival Etched doubles its value to $10.3B in seven months
blurb: Etched raised $300M in a Series C that values the inference-chip startup at $10.3B, double its $5B mark from December and its total funding past $1B. The bet is specialization: chips built only for transformer inference, run at under half the usual voltage to beat the heat throttling that caps Nvidia's general-purpose GPUs. That the round pulled in SK Hynix, the top AI-memory maker, signals where the moat is forming, and Google hardwiring Gemini into its Frozen v2 chip shows the fixed-architecture idea is going mainstream. The unglamorous part starts now: moving from working prototypes to mass production.

title: Sierra acquires Takeoff, which develops "long-horizon" agents, for an undisclosed sum; founded in 2025, Takeoff neared $10M in annualized revenue in early July (The Information)
blurb: Sierra acquired Takeoff, a maker of "long-horizon" AI agents, for an undisclosed sum, less than a year after Takeoff was founded in 2025. By early July the startup had already neared $10M in annualized revenue, the kind of trajectory that now gets a company bought before it turns two. The pattern repeats across AI: incumbents are paying up to absorb small, fast-growing teams building agents that run complex tasks over long time horizons, rather than waiting for them to mature into rivals.

title: £45m secondary share sale makes Moneybox a unicorn
blurb: Moneybox hit a roughly £800 million valuation through a £45m secondary share sale on the London Stock Exchange's Private Securities Market, about 45% above its 2024 mark. No new capital was raised. The deal let decade-long employees cash out shares to institutional investors led by Apis, while the business itself is compounding: more than £115m in 2025 revenue, a third straight profitable year, 1.9m customers, and £23bn in assets under administration. The secondary route is becoming a real liquidity path for mature private companies whose staff want cash without an IPO.

title: Alphabet’s Anthropic Stake Jumps to Around $124 Billion
blurb: Alphabet's stake in Anthropic has climbed to around $124 billion, one of the most lucrative bets in the company's history. The position now rivals the value of entire Big Tech divisions, built not on an acquisition but on early capital into a startup Alphabet also competes with through Gemini. For founders, it underscores how the frontier-lab landscape now runs on cross-ownership, where the hyperscalers backing your suppliers are also the rivals building against them.

title: Vertical AI Market Map 2026: Israeli Startups and Funding Rounds
blurb: Israel's H1 2026 funding data is where the long-predicted vertical AI thesis first shows up in the numbers, with billions raised but the more telling signal in where application-layer capital landed. Banks, insurers, defence groups, property managers, pharma firms, and gaming studios are funding startups that underwrite policies, manage loans, discover drugs, and run workflows end to end, not just add an AI interface on top. That is the line between vertical SaaS and vertical AI: the old software helped professionals organize their work, while this generation is being paid to perform it.

title: Google just had its first negative cash flow quarter due to massive AI spending
blurb: Google posted its first-ever quarter of negative free cash flow, spending $44.9B on AI infrastructure against $39.1B in operating cash income to land at -$5.8B. Revenue was fine, $119.8B for Q2 2026 with Cloud up 23.8% on AI demand, but the company raised its 2026 capex guidance to as much as $205B, up from the $180B to $190B it had signaled and more than double last year's $91B. The signal for founders is that even the most profitable ad business on earth is now willing to run cash-negative to hold its place in the AI buildout.

title: The AI bill is the easy part. The hard part is everything it changed
blurb: The conversation this June flipped from token maxing to token cutting, with Meta, Uber, Walmart, and Amazon all capping employee AI usage after Uber burned through its 2026 AI budget in four months. But capping tokens is an input lever, not an output measure, and it dodges the CFO's actual question of what the spend produced. The deeper problem is that AI is the first category of labor enterprises have ever bought with no system of record, so the same workflow lands in COGS and OpEx at once while the org chart and P&L stay stuck describing a workforce that no longer exists.

title: Travis Kalanick’s industrial automation startup Atoms raises $1.7B in funding
blurb: Travis Kalanick raised $1.7B for Atoms, an industrial automation holding company led by the Uber co-founder and backed by Andreessen Horowitz, Uber, and bank debt. The company bundles seven business units, most spun out of his City Storage Systems, spanning ghost kitchens (CloudKitchens), robotic food assembly (Lab37), restaurant software (Otter), and autonomous mining trucks (Pronto AI). The bet is that the same automate-the-manual-labor playbook works across restaurants, mining, and transportation, and investors are funding a conglomerate rather than a single product before most of it has proven out.

title: Startup founders urge U.S. government not to shut off Chinese open weight AI
blurb: Startup founders are lobbying the U.S. government to keep Chinese open weight models like DeepSeek and Qwen legally available, arguing a ban would cut off the cheap, tunable foundation many of them build on. The open weight models coming out of China now rival U.S. frontier labs on price and often on quality, and thousands of American products already run on them. Restricting access would hand a moat to a handful of closed U.S. labs and raise the cost floor for every startup that can't afford to train its own.

title: Computer and browser use in Codex (5 real examples)
blurb: Codex's browser and computer use let the ChatGPT desktop app plus Chrome extension drive real workflows, not just answer questions. This walkthrough shows five in production: QA-testing an onboarding flow that surfaced 11 issues including a high-severity blocker, persona-impersonation testing that exposes friction the builder can't see, clearing a LinkedIn inbox hands-free, and running an AI personal shopper. The counterintuitive lesson is that under-prompting frontier models beats detailed step-by-step instructions.

title: Netflix kauft für fast 600 Millionen Ben Afflecks KI-Startup
blurb: Netflix acquired InterPositive, the AI post-production startup co-founded by Ben Affleck, for $587M in cash, with Affleck and the full team joining as senior advisors. The tooling fixes missing shots, swaps backgrounds, and corrects lighting errors, work that used to burn weeks of manual VFX labor. Around 300 Netflix films and series already use generative AI in production or post, so the deal reads less like an experiment and more like a studio buying the pipeline it plans to run at scale.

title: Patreon lays off 20% of its workforce
blurb: Patreon laid off 20% of its staff, or 93 people, its largest cut since 2022, even as CEO Jack Conte told employees the core business is strong. Conte was explicit that AI is not replacing the roles, arguing the tools don't substitute for human creativity and connection, but he tied the restructuring to how AI has reshaped the way the company works and organizes. The distinction matters for operators watching this wave of cuts. AI doesn't need to do your team's job to shrink your headcount. It just has to change the shape of the work.

title: AegisAI, founded by former Google security execs, lands $36M to stop AI-driven spear phishing
blurb: AegisAI, founded by former Google security execs Cy Khormaee and Ryan Luo, raised a $36M Series A led by Battery Ventures to defend email against AI-crafted spear phishing. The pitch is that rule-based, if-then filters can't keep up now that attackers use AI to research a target and write perfectly bespoke messages, which the founders say bypass existing controls more than half the time. Less than a year in, dozens of customers including LangChain, Mesh, and Lokker have signed on, a sign that AI-versus-AI security is becoming its own line item rather than a feature of legacy tools.

title: Tesla’s robotaxis are moving in reverse
blurb: Tesla's paid Robotaxi miles fell about 36% quarter-over-quarter, from roughly 1.1 million in Q1 to around 700,000 in Q2, even as the company expanded to six cities across Texas and Florida. The drop runs directly against Musk's "balls to the wall for autonomy" framing and lands alongside weak core earnings that sent the stock down more than 13%. On the earnings call, Musk said Tesla can't scale until it accumulates driving data specific to the purpose-built Cybercab, which signals the low-cost fleet that underwrites much of Tesla's valuation is years, not quarters, away.

title: Flush with AI cash, South Korean groups are making their biggest US investment push in years, with their Q1 US foreign direct investment up 100%+ YoY to $10.2B (Financial Times)
blurb: South Korean conglomerates are pouring their AI windfall into the US, lifting Q1 foreign direct investment more than 100% year over year to $10.2B. The push is their biggest US commitment in years, aimed at chips, batteries, and manufacturing capacity close to American customers and away from tariff risk. For founders, it signals where the next wave of capital, supply-chain partnerships, and industrial buildout is landing, and Korean strategics are increasingly the check writers behind it.

title: Dassault Systemes to Buy ArisGlobal for Up to $2 Billion
blurb: Dassault Systèmes agreed to buy U.S. life-sciences software maker ArisGlobal for up to $2B, adding drug-safety and regulatory tools to its portfolio. The move is squarely about AI, as Dassault looks to embed automation into the pharmacovigilance and compliance workflows its biotech and pharma customers already run. Vertical software incumbents are now buying their way into applied AI rather than building it, and the premium is flowing to companies that own regulated, data-heavy workflows where accuracy is non-negotiable.

title: The AI compute gap: Enterprises are buying infrastructure faster than they can measure what it costs
blurb: Enterprise AI infrastructure spending is now running well ahead of the ability to measure what it costs, according to a survey of 107 companies. Only 21% run AI in production at scale, yet 83% report GPU utilization at 50% or less and fewer than half rigorously track what their compute actually costs. Churn intent is high, with 64% planning to switch or add an infrastructure provider within a year and buyers deciding on integration and total cost of ownership rather than token price. The result is heavy, fast-moving investment outrunning the visibility needed to control it.

title: Singapore-based Ropedia, which captures real-world human experience via video and converts it into model-ready multimodal datasets, raised a $22M pre-Series A (Kyt Dotson/SiliconANGLE)
blurb: Robotics and world-model labs have squeezed most of the value out of text and are now paying for something harder to get: real-world human experience captured on video. Ropedia, a Singapore-based startup that films physical tasks and converts the footage into model-ready multimodal datasets, raised a $22M pre-Series A on exactly that bet. As frontier training runs into the ceiling of public text data, the next scarce input is proprietary sensor and video data of humans doing things, and the companies that own the capture pipeline get to sell it to every lab that needs it.

title: AI chip startup Etched more than doubles valuation to $10.3B in new $300M round
blurb: Etched raised $300M in a Sequoia-led Series C, more than doubling its valuation to $10.3B in roughly seven months. The bet is narrow: a chip built only for inference, not training, which Etched says lets it sidestep the thermal ceiling that forces general-purpose GPUs to throttle their clock speeds. SK Hynix joining alongside a16z and Jane Street signals that even memory giants are hedging against an inference market that Nvidia currently dominates but no longer owns by default.

title: Reddit may cut Google off from its content, and it isn’t the only one
blurb: The 20-year bargain where publishers let Google crawl in exchange for traffic is breaking, and Reddit is the loudest crack. Reddit shares fell about 9% after reports it may not renew its $60M-a-year AI training deal with Google on the old terms, and it wants the next one priced by usage. The pressure is Google's AI Overviews, which answer the query and kill the click. Between June 2025 and 2026, Google referral traffic fell 23% at Politico and more than 85% at Business Insider, and USA Today, Reuters, and The Economist are all reweighing their deals too.

title: Monday.com cuts 20% of its workforce to restructure for the AI era
blurb: Monday.com is cutting about 20% of its workforce, roughly 620 people, and framing it not as a margin play but as a bet on flatter teams built around AI agents and customer implementation. Co-CEO Eran Zinman says the vast majority of the savings will be reinvested into talent, products, and AI, with fewer management layers and smaller autonomous teams. A healthy software company shedding one-fifth of its staff to restructure around AI signals where the labor math is heading: even growing vendors now see their pre-AI org chart as the wrong shape for the next chapter.

title: Google pays Reddit $60M a year. For what, exactly?
blurb: Reddit is weighing whether to restrict Google's access to its content as the two renegotiate a 2024 AI data-licensing deal worth roughly $60M a year, and Reddit's stock dropped as much as 8% on the news. The leverage is real: Reddit is the most-cited domain in ChatGPT answers at 847K+ mentions, nearly double Wikipedia, and it accounts for around a third of external citations on unbranded discovery queries like "best CRM for startups." That is bottom-of-funnel real estate, the moment a buyer asks a machine who to trust, and one platform shapes most of the answer.

title: When and When Not to Raise
blurb: Venture capital is jet fuel, not gas for everyone, and the best investors will tell you when your business is a motorcycle. A profitable agency throwing off $1M a year in cash, run with people you like, alongside time with your family, is often a great business that VC would only wreck. Raise when your company is built to become a jet, one where outside capital compounds into a much larger outcome. If the honest answer is that you already have what you want, taking the money just adds pressure to fly a plane you never wanted to build.

title: The Forward Deployed Engineer Playbookhe Forward Deployed Engineer Playbook
blurb: The forward-deployed engineer is the role every AI company is now racing to hire, because the best ones turn a raw capability into a business outcome and carry the customer until they can run it alone. They are scarce and expensive, and they resist a standard interview rubric, which is exactly why most teams staff them badly. Insight Partners pulled FDE leaders from ServiceNow, Databricks, Workato, and Wonderful into one room, and the playbook turns their agreement into a copyable operating manual: the charter, interview loop, comp bands, pod model, 90-day runbook, and bench budget math.

title: YC-backed telli raises €13.1 million to build AI for B2C customer operations
blurb: Berlin-based telli raised €13.1M ($15M) Seed led by redalpine to build AI that runs B2C customer-facing operations across every channel.

title: How to Win the Largest Market in AI
blurb: AI inference is becoming the largest market in computing, and the a16z argument is that whoever builds hardware in the shape of that workload wins it. Training is capex you pay once per model, but inference is opex that scales with every token served, making it the actual COGS of intelligence. History says that once a workload grows large enough and its shape stabilizes, the economics push it off general-purpose chips and into custom silicon, the same turn that gave us the GPU and Broadcom's switch chips, and inference is now hitting that threshold.

title: kausable raises €12M to rethink how AI learns
blurb: kausable, a European frontier AI lab spun out of Heidelberg University with ties to Black Forest Labs, raised €12M in seed funding led by UVC Partners and Entourage. The pitch is reasoning-first models that adapt to changing context without the constant, costly retraining today's systems require. The bet lands on the sovereignty trend, with angels from Black Forest Labs, OpenAI, and DeepMind backing a frontier stack built in Europe rather than imported from US labs.

title: Wall Street is treating Nvidia GPUs like aircraft collateral — and a $35B syndication is quietly testing whether the credit markets agree
blurb: Wall Street is now underwriting Nvidia GPUs the way it underwrites aircraft, and a $35B debt package tied to AI chip infrastructure is quietly being syndicated across insurers, credit funds, and CLO managers to test whether the credit markets buy it. Hyperscalers and compute providers are increasingly funding their Nvidia procurement with debt rather than equity, letting pension savings ride the AI capex cycle without touching operator equity. The open question is depreciation: these are semiconductors on a few-year replacement cycle being collateralized like assets that throw off cash for decades.

title: OpenAI and Datadog leaders back AI deployment startup Arrakis
blurb: Arrakis, a London startup building AI agents for industrial companies, emerged from stealth with $38m raised in just over three months, including a $30m Series A led by Blossom Capital with backing from Datadog's Olivier Pomel and OpenAI's Olivier Godement. Its bet is that most companies can run chatbots but stall when they try to put AI to work on real operations, so it embeds forward-deployed engineers to train and ship model-agnostic agents on a client's own data in weeks. The customer list already includes NYSE-listed enterprises across energy, logistics, and industrials.

title: The Rise And Rise Of Billion-Dollar-Plus Rounds
blurb: Venture funding has flipped to megadeal territory, with 60% of global startup dollars this year, roughly $320 billion, going to rounds of $1 billion or more. In the U.S. the tilt is sharper at 73%, and just two rounds, OpenAI and Anthropic, account for more than half of that $290 billion. The deals are also getting more frequent, with 23 known billion-dollar-plus U.S. rounds already matching last year's record with five months left, and nearly all of them are late-stage rather than the seed bets venture once meant.

title: From MIT research to $1.5B unicorn: Ethan Hu’s Meshy raises $400M for AI-powered 3D creation
blurb: Meshy raised nearly $400M at a $1.5B valuation, the largest round any dedicated AI-3D company has pulled, on the claim that it compresses 3D asset creation from weeks of specialist work to about a minute at roughly $1 per model. The traction backs it up, with more than 12 million registered users, over 100 million generated models, and ARR up roughly 12-fold year over year. The more telling pattern sits in the cap table: nearly every major AI-3D round in 2026, Meshy's and rival Tripo AI's included, has been funded almost entirely by China-linked capital.

title: Led By DeepSeek, 10 Frontier Labs Rush Onto The Crunchbase Unicorn Board In June
blurb: Ten of the 34 companies that joined the Crunchbase Unicorn Board in June were frontier AI labs, collectively worth $65 billion, led by DeepSeek at a $50 billion valuation off its first external raise. The new labs cluster around new bets: neuroscience-based architectures, physics and robotics models, sovereign AI, and open source. Yet the board's total value fell by more than $1 trillion the same month, as SpaceX went public and took Cursor with it via a $60 billion acquisition. The unicorn count is climbing while the paper wealth exits through the top.

title: Clipway, the London private equity firm founded by 3 ex-Ardian executives, just closed a record $6.4B debut fund
blurb: Clipway, a London secondaries firm founded three years ago by three ex-Ardian executives, closed its debut fund at $6.4B, 60% above its original $4B target and the largest first-time secondaries platform ever raised. What backed the pedigree was a machine: the firm's AI platform, TESS, screened more than $267B of secondary opportunities and rejected 97% of them, deploying just over $6B across 1,403 companies and 177 funds. The signal for founders is that even in relationship-driven private equity, LPs are now writing record checks on the strength of proprietary data and automated selection.

title: The most valuable thing Apple built in the 2000s wasn't the iPod
blurb: iTunes, not the iPod, was Apple's most consequential build of the 2000s. Its 99¢ per-track store launched in April 2003 with about 200,000 songs, hit its six-month sales forecast in six days, and carried more than 70% of legal digital downloads within three years. It worked because Apple stacked strategic levers rather than just selling songs online, and in the process it unbundled the album and set up the streaming and playlist economy we live in now.

title: Google Study Says AI Is Helping Workers, Not Replacing Them
blurb: Google's new research argues AI is augmenting workers rather than displacing them, with most usage concentrated in drafting, analysis, and coding assistance instead of headcount cuts. The finding lands amid rising fear that AI is already hollowing out entry-level jobs, and it comes from the company that builds Gemini and profits from adoption. For founders, the signal is that AI's near-term return shows up as leverage on the people you already have, not as a smaller payroll.

title: Menlo Ventures’ Matt Murphy explains what AI startups founders must do differently
blurb: Anthropic hit a $47B revenue run rate by May, up from $9B in 2025, growth Menlo's Matt Murphy says he hasn't seen in 25 years of investing. Murphy's read is that the model was never the moat. Claude Code, MCP, and Claude Skills turned Anthropic from a strong model into a platform, and that platform layer is what let companies like Lovable and Legora scale faster than anything he's backed before.

title: Travis is Back
blurb: a16z is backing Travis Kalanick's Atoms, a company he has quietly built for eight years to bring specialized robots into atoms-heavy industries like manufacturing, logistics, and storage. The bet is that "industrial AI" repeats what Uber did for transportation: most of the world's work still happens in the physical world, and that's where the next frontier of productivity sits. Marc Andreessen is joining the board, signaling that the firm sees specialized robots, not humanoids, as the inevitable path to digitizing physical labor.

title: Instagram challenger Yope raises £9.2m for algorithm-free social media
blurb: Yope raised $12.3M in a pre-Series A round led by Northzone to scale an ad-free, algorithm-free alternative to Meta, TikTok, and X. The London app confines sharing to closed groups of close friends and family, then uses generative AI agents to compile the photos into curated recaps and private memory archives. It claims more than 14 million users and half a billion shared photos and videos, and the bet is that consumers increasingly want small private spaces over public feeds, with AI as the layer that makes those spaces feel alive.

title: Rente in Startups investieren? Der neue Plan der Bundesregierung
blurb: Germany's economy minister Katherina Reiche wants to route part of the country's pension system into venture capital, using the coming reform to raise contributions two percentage points and channel the money into funds. The plan would push retirement savings toward startup financing, a source of patient domestic capital that European tech has long lacked. If it passes, German founders gain a structural funding base to draw on, and the rest of Europe gets a template for turning pension pools into venture money rather than watching the best rounds get led from the US.

title: Vor diesem 34-jährigen Chinesen zittert das Silicon Valley
blurb: China's frontier labs keep closing the gap, and the latest proof is Kimi K3, an open-weight model from Moonshot AI that matches OpenAI and Anthropic's best on coding and agent tasks at a fraction of the cost. The founder behind it is Yang Zhilin, a 34-year-old Carnegie Mellon PhD who turned down US tech offers to build in China. The pattern that matters for operators: top-tier capability is arriving as cheap open weights, so the premium you pay for a closed frontier model now buys a shrinking edge.

title: Why Software Factories Fail (or: harness engineering is not enough)
blurb: The author argues that "software factory" setups where no human reads or writes code fail not because harness and loop engineering are weak, but because removing human review lets defects compound. He cites a Faros AI report showing PR review quality dropping, incidents rising, and bugs per developer climbing since teams adopted AI coding tools, while cautioning that the data is a correlation signal rather than proof.

title: Durable Objects are Made for Agents
blurb: The author makes the case that Cloudflare Durable Objects, which pair an on-demand V8 isolate with a per-object SQLite instance and ID-based request routing, map cleanly onto agent workloads that need per-session state and event-driven execution via alarms. The post covers where the primitive shines for building agents and where it falls short, drawing on several months of building almost exclusively on DOs.

title: I Inspected My Take-Home Interview Project. It Was a Whole Operation
blurb: A developer accepted a take-home assignment from a LinkedIn recruiter and found a malicious payload hidden in the project's `.git/hooks` directory, disguised behind a clean `requirements.txt` and a legitimate-looking FastAPI codebase. Running `tree -a` surfaced the operation that a casual clone-and-run would have executed silently, a reminder that git hooks fire on standard commands and warrant inspection before you touch an untrusted repo.

title: Characterizing Metastable Faults and Failures
blurb: This paper offers the first analytical causal account of metastable failures, the self-sustaining goodput collapse that persists via internal feedback loops like retries and queues even after the trigger disappears. It frames a metastable fault as a "sin of composition" between individually stable components wired into a destructive cycle and ties recovery to scheduling, though the author disputes its claim that metastable fault tolerance can be designed through local pairwise reasoning alone.

title: Everyone should know SIMD
blurb: Mitchell Hashimoto argues that SIMD is far more approachable than its reputation suggests, since the common "process N values at a time" pattern that speeds up a naive for loop almost always follows the same five-step shape. Using Zig examples that generalize to any language, the post shows how to broadcast constants, loop over vector-width chunks, and hit 4x to 8x speedups on large byte arrays without the complexity of libraries like simdjson.

title: Sierra MCP Gateway Dev Diary
blurb: Sierra's AI acceleration team built an internal MCP gateway to give agents safe, audited access to the SaaS products and internal systems the company runs on, replacing per-team integrations with one shared permissioning and auditing layer. The development diary tracks the project from a single service with one weekly user through the depth that emerged from a supposedly "boring" mature technology, built by roughly one-and-a-half engineers over the spring.

title: GigaToken: ~1000x faster Language model tokenization
blurb: GigaToken is a drop-in tokenizer for language modeling that claims roughly 1000x higher throughput than HuggingFace's tokenizers, hitting GB/s encoding on a single 11.9GB OpenWebText file. It ships a Rust core with compatibility shims for HuggingFace and tiktoken, though matching their exact output costs some speed, so the full 1000x only lands when you use the native Gigatoken API that reads files directly and parallelizes the whole encode.

title: Aurora DSQL: Scalable, Multi-Region OLTP
blurb: This paper describes Amazon's Aurora DSQL, a distributed SQL database that disaggregates the transaction, journal, and storage layers to scale writes and reads independently while keeping PostgreSQL compatibility. It relies on optimistic concurrency control with a single commit-time conflict check, and provides strongly consistent active-active multi-region deployments backed by a quorum-replicated journal rather than synchronous cross-region locking.

title: Never Enough
blurb: Armin Ronacher argues that Silicon Valley's AI race has curdled from rebellion into fear, pointing to a couple earning $550,000 who feel behind because an Anthropic employee might buy the dream house first, and a founder who has Claude grade her recorded first dates. His thesis is that people afraid of being replaced by machines respond by handing those machines their judgment, attention, and time, trading every saved hour back into a race with no finish line.

title: The case for a cooldown: Why Dependabot now waits before issuing version updates
blurb: Dependabot now waits at least three days after a release is published before opening non-security version update pull requests, a default cooldown you can override in dependabot.yml. The change targets supply chain attacks like September 2025's compromise of chalk, debug, and other packages, where poisoned versions were live for roughly two hours, long enough for update tooling to pull them into build pipelines before anyone caught the malware.

title: Automating cross-repo documentation with GitHub Agentic Workflows
blurb: The Aspire team built cross-repo documentation automation using GitHub Agentic Workflows, turning merged product pull requests in microsoft/aspire into SME-reviewed docs pull requests in the separate microsoft/aspire.dev repo without granting agents broad write-everywhere tokens. For Aspire 13.3 and 13.4, the setup shipped 82 feature-docs pull requests at a median of 44.8 hours after the product pull request, each reviewed by the engineer who wrote the feature.

title: Context Matters: Improving the Practical Reliability of LLM-Based Unit Test Generation
blurb: CATGen is a context-aware workflow for LLM-based unit test generation that treats compilation reliability as an engineering problem rather than a prompting one, making project dependencies explicit, building deterministic test skeletons, and swapping iterative LLM repair for lightweight static analysis. On proprietary industrial projects and Defects4J, it raises compilation success and structural coverage while cutting generation time and token consumption against existing LLM-based approaches.

title: Cisco’s new AI model tells code reviewers where to look for vulnerabilities
blurb: Cisco released Antares, a family of open-weight models (350M, 1B, and 3B parameters) that take a CWE description and return a ranked shortlist of repository files most likely to contain that class of vulnerability. Unlike rule-based static analyzers like Semgrep or CodeQL, it acts as an exploration agent, and Cisco says the 3B model approaches GPT-5.5 on its internal Vloc benchmark while running locally.

title: PerfAgent: Profiler-Guided Iterative Refinement for Repository-Level Code Optimization
blurb: PerfAgent wraps an off-the-shelf coding agent in a profiler-guided, verifier-in-the-loop workflow, using profiler evidence rather than raw timing to find real hotspots and push past the first passing patch on repository-level optimization tasks. It more than doubles the rate of expert-matching patches over OpenHands with GPT-5.1, reaching 39.2% on GSO and 74% on SWE-fficiency-Lite while beating an oracle best-of-five baseline at lower cost.

title: Copilot vs. raw API access: What are you actually paying for?
blurb: GitHub argues the Copilot-versus-API question is really about which layer you want to own: raw API access if you're building a product feature with your own prompts, retrieval, routing, and logs, and Copilot if you want the issue-to-reviewed-PR workflow already wired across the editor, repo, terminal, and org policies. Cost per task depends on context selection, tool use, and retries, not just the listed token rate.

title: Not just development, distribution of software may change as well
blurb: antirez argues that AI coding tools change not just how software is written but how it is distributed, since users can now modify and specialize the code they receive rather than waiting on a polished stable release. He suggests the stable-versus-unstable branch model may give way to repositories treated as templates, where work too unproven for the general public is exactly right for a subset of users tuning it to their own hardware and requirements.

title: So Reddit has decided that plain HTML is unsafe
blurb: Reddit now requires a login to view old.reddit.com while leaving the logged-out New Reddit accessible, framing the change as a defense against abusive scraping and automated traffic. The author argues the inconsistency undermines the stated safety rationale and reads instead as an effort to gate human-written content, the same corpus that made appending "site:reddit.com" a reliable way to surface genuine answers in the LLM age.

title: How Our Universal Content Processing Platform Riviera Evolved for AI and Beyond
blurb: Dropbox opened up Riviera, the content processing platform that has handled file previews, transcoding, and format conversion across its products for roughly a decade, via a public API and MCP tools. The post covers how Riviera grew from an internal preview service into a shared platform spanning Search, Replay, Sign, and Dash, the architecture behind supporting 300+ file formats, and why it now targets developers preparing documents for AI applications.

title: Maintenance Signals in AI-Assisted GitHub Repositories: Evidence from GenAI Adopters
blurb: This paper analyzes maintenance signals across 622 GenAI adopters, 179 AI-assisted GitHub repositories, and 248 issues, matched against 179 traditional repos. The authors find AI-assisted projects carry longer READMEs and shift maintenance work toward verifying generated code and managing external AI dependencies like API rate limits and provider outages.

title: Model Context Protocol prepares to break with its stateful past
blurb: The Model Context Protocol is landing its biggest revision since launch, dropping stateful sessions in favor of a stateless request model and cutting several rarely used features. The overhaul simplifies the spec for hosted servers but leaves teams running homebrew implementations facing a substantial migration to keep their integrations working.

title: 10 REM"_(C2SLFF4
blurb: A developer decodes the mysterious `"_(C2SLFF4` REM comment in a 1980 BASIC game as Z80 machine code for PRNG seeding.

title: If You Think You Can Do Real-World Text-to-SQL
blurb: The author argues that real-world text-to-SQL is far harder than benchmark scores imply, because production databases carry cryptic column names, undocumented business logic, and ambiguous natural-language questions that benchmarks like Spider never capture. The post breaks down failure modes including schema linking errors, unstated join conditions, and semantic mismatches, arguing that reliable systems need domain context and human-in-the-loop validation rather than a stronger model alone.

title: Delivery, Not Storage: Cue-Anchored Working Memory as a Harness Property for Coding Agents
blurb: This paper argues that coding agents need a second memory tier beyond authored documents: situationally-cued operational facts that the harness injects deterministically rather than leaving the agent to write and retrieve on its own. In a controlled run the agent performed zero voluntary memory operations across 114 turns, and facts held only in conversation vanished at the first compaction, while harness-injected memories survived all 138 compact-resumes intact.

title: Software rendering in 500 lines of bare C++
blurb: This tutorial series builds a software renderer in about 500 lines of bare C++, implementing line and triangle rasterization from scratch to show how OpenGL, Vulkan, Metal, and DirectX work under the hood. It uses no third-party graphics libraries, starting from a single TGA image class that can only set individual pixels, with students typically reaching a working renderer in 10 to 20 hours.

title: Show HN: Bento - An entire PowerPoint in one HTML file (edit+view+data+collab)
blurb: Bento packs a full presentation tool into a single HTML file, handling editing, viewing, embedded data, and real-time collaboration without a backend or app install. The self-contained format means a deck travels as one portable file you can open, edit, or share anywhere a browser runs, trading cloud sync for zero infrastructure.

title: Software Factories, Light and Dark
blurb: Addy Osmani argues that AI-assisted development is producing "software factories" whose light and dark sides hinge on whether teams retain control over ideas rather than every line of generated code. His central shift is from tech debt to knowledge debt, where the human role becomes owning the consequences of decisions instead of reviewing output line by line.

title: Visual Studio Code 1.130 shines on Agents window
blurb: Microsoft released Visual Studio Code 1.130, which overhauls the Agents window for running parallel agent sessions with file-level diff statistics and a more compact multi-file diff view. The update adds assisted permissions in the agent host, where the LLM evaluates each tool call's risk and decides whether to run it or require approval, plus clickable file links in Git diff output in the terminal.

title: New pip flag fixes longstanding Python frustration
blurb: pip 26.2 adds `pip install --only-deps`, which installs a package's runtime dependencies without installing the package itself, closing a years-old gap that previously forced manual dependency extraction or full builds. The flag covers runtime requirements but not build dependencies, and mirrors behavior already available in third-party tools like uv's `uv sync --no-install-project`. It ships by the end of July 2026.

title: WSL container: A quiet revolution for Windows development
blurb: Microsoft shipped WSL-native container support at the end of June, giving Windows a container platform that runs without Docker Desktop, Hyper-V VMs, or the virtualization layers the author found slow and unstable. The new CLI covers the full container lifecycle and reflects a broader Build 2026 push to make Windows a viable developer platform, driven by the reality that over 50% of Azure servers run Linux.

title: The startup's Postgres survival guide
blurb: A Hatchet engineer distilled two years of production Postgres experience into a survival guide for startups, covering schema design, the query planner, bulk updates, autovacuum, and advanced tuning. The doc assumes SQL basics and leans on practical rules of thumb over formal normalization, noting that ORMs block many scaling optimizations unless you can drop to raw SQL via tools like Prisma TypedSQL or sqlc.

title: G# language for .NET borrows from Go, Kotlin, and Swift
blurb: G# is a new pre-1.0 language for .NET that brings Go, Kotlin, and Swift ergonomics to the CLR, compiling to managed PE assemblies that work with the standard .NET toolchain. It offers Go-style keywords like func, structs, slices, channels, and select alongside nullable flow, structured concurrency, and direct calls into any BCL, NuGet, or first-party .NET type, with a VS Code extension already shipping syntax highlighting and language server support.

title: Towards Reliable C-to-Rust Translation with Rule-Guided Reasoning and Reinforcement Learning
blurb: TRAVEL is a C-to-Rust translation framework that pairs Rust-rule-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search with reinforcement learning that couples execution feedback with reasoning-quality signals, targeting the syntax violations and semantic drift that trip up LLM-based migration. On xCodeEval it beats the strongest prompting baseline by 26.22% on computational accuracy and 18.77% on compilation success, and on Huawei and Linux-kernel benchmarks it cuts the unsafe-code rate by about 13%.

title: Show HN: Palmier Pro – Open-source macOS video editor built for AI
blurb: Palmier Pro is an open-source macOS video editor written in Swift that exposes an MCP server, letting Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor generate and edit clips directly in the timeline alongside an in-app agent. The editor is free and GPLv3, with generative models like Seedance, Kling, and Nano Banana Pro gated behind a paid login; it runs only on macOS 26 Tahoe on Apple Silicon.

title: Agentic coding goes hands-free as OpenAI brings GPT-Live's full duplex voice control to Codex and ChatGPT on the desktop
blurb: OpenAI brought GPT-Live's full-duplex voice model to the ChatGPT desktop app on macOS and Windows, letting developers orchestrate Codex jobs, review pull requests, and debug by voice while background models like GPT-5.5 handle the reasoning. The architecture decouples the real-time voice layer from execution, and on macOS adds screen context so ChatGPT Voice can read the frontmost window, local files, and codebase structure while agents run tasks asynchronously.

title: JEP 540: Simple JSON API (Now in Incubator)
blurb: JEP 540 defines a standard JSON parsing and generation API for the JDK, letting developers handle RFC 8259 documents without pulling in Jackson, Gson, or another external library. The incubating API deliberately stays minimal, covering only strict RFC 8259 conformance and skipping data binding, streaming, and syntax extensions, so it complements rather than replaces established libraries for exploratory reads and simple extraction.

title: Learn WebGPU for C++
blurb: This documentation teaches you to build native 3D applications in C++ using the WebGPU graphics API, targeting Windows, Linux, and macOS from scratch. Chapters progress from a Hello Triangle through basic 3D rendering, compute, and advanced techniques, with code available in both a C++-styled webgpu.hpp wrapper and the raw C webgpu.h API, backed by either wgpu-native or Dawn.

title: Claude Code Can Now Build and Test iOS Apps in Apple's Simulator
blurb: Anthropic released an iOS Simulator integration for Claude Code Desktop on macOS, now in public beta, that lets Claude build, run, and watch apps live in a simulator pane and iterate until the project works. The pane drives the simulator directly rather than through computer use, so it skips the Accessibility and Screen Recording permissions, though it requires Xcode and works in local sessions only.

title: Cruller: Bun's Zig Runtime, Continued on Zig 0.16
blurb: Cruller is a fork of the last Zig-based Bun release, stripped to just the pieces needed to run pre-built production JavaScript servers and ported to vanilla Zig 0.16. It keeps JavaScriptCore, Bun.serve, HTTP/1-3, WebSockets, fetch, and the module resolver while dropping the package manager, bundler, transpiler, test runner, and N-API, yielding an 18% smaller runtime (73.0 MiB vs Bun 1.3.14's 88.5 MiB) at roughly benchmark parity on Linux x64.

title: Cursor Router
blurb: Cursor released Cursor Router, a model router that picks between frontier and price-efficient models per request based on whether the user optimizes for Intelligence, Balance, or Cost. Early-access customers saw no quality drop-off at a lower cost per commit versus routing everything to Opus 4.8, and admins on Teams and Enterprise plans can allow or block specific models and set per-team defaults.

title: Your JS Date Is Lying to You
blurb: This post catalogs how JavaScript's Date API silently corrupts date logic: `new Date('2026-07-21')` parses as UTC midnight and shifts a day west of UTC, month arguments are 0-indexed, `setDate(32)` rolls over without error, and identical-looking strings parse twelve hours apart depending on the separator. It walks through parsing, mutation, timezone, and arithmetic traps, then shows how the Temporal API fixes each one, plus safe patterns for code still stuck on Date.

title: I turned a photo of my handwriting into a real font, then open-sourced the whole pipeline
blurb: draw-your-font is an MIT-licensed pipeline that turns a single photo of your handwriting into an installable TTF, running locally through pure npm with no system dependencies like FontForge or potrace. It ships as three layers, an npm library, a CLI, and a Claude Code skill that uses vision to find and label your letters, build the font, and critique its own tracing before you review it.

title: Introducing Ghost Cut - or why Cut & Paste is broken everywhere
blurb: The author argues that cut & paste has been quietly broken for decades: a cut can't be fully undone because the clipboard change persists, it reflows the document the instant you cut, and the move it represents takes two undo steps rather than one. Their fix, Ghost Cut, fades the selected text and makes it inert on ctrl+x without touching the clipboard, then removes the ghosted span only on paste, keeping the whole move atomic and escape-reversible.

title: The State of WebMCP
blurb: WebMCP lets a web page register typed JavaScript functions that browser AI agents call directly, replacing DOM scraping and vision-based clicking with named tools carrying JSON input schemas. The W3C draft has Google and Microsoft as editors and a live Chrome origin trial, but site adoption is near zero and no mainstream agent consumes the tools yet, with the API already moving from navigator to document and creating early migration debt.

title: Microsoft lands Visual Studio Code 1.130 with agent rearchitecturing
blurb: Microsoft released Visual Studio Code 1.130, a weekly update that rearchitects the editor's agent system to make agentic coding sessions more reliable and extensible. The overhaul restructures how agents plan, invoke tools, and manage context, laying groundwork for third-party agent integrations alongside the built-in Copilot workflows.

title: Fable just found a 15-30% memory efficiency improvement in Turbopack/Next.js
blurb: Fable found a 15-30% memory efficiency improvement in Turbopack, Next.js's Rust-based bundler, and documented the fix in a thread walking through the profiling and allocation changes. The gain comes from reducing redundant allocations in the module graph, cutting peak memory during builds without a corresponding change to compile times.

title: Connect your AI tools to Clerk MCP with one command
blurb: The Clerk CLI's new `clerk mcp install` command registers the Clerk MCP server across ten AI clients, including Claude Code, Cursor, and VS Code, from a single interactive prompt with no per-client config editing. It replaces the `npx mcp-remote` setup with a built-in stdio-to-HTTP bridge, so updating the CLI updates the connection everywhere and re-running install always converges without conflict state.

title: Optimizing for Almost Sorted Data: Sort Pushdown in Apache DataFusion
blurb: Apache DataFusion now exploits sortedness automatically, even when data is only partially sorted or its ordering was never declared. The engine discovers a global sort order from per-file min/max statistics, deletes redundant sorts at plan time, and biases scan order toward the most-promising files, with dynamic filters driving row-group pruning at runtime. The payoff is ORDER BY ... LIMIT queries that skip the blocking full sort and full scan, cutting latency and peak memory on large tables.

title: To Every Agent Its Own Database
blurb: Joe Reis inverts the "make the warehouse agent-ready" approach, arguing that scaling shared analytical platforms with bigger clusters and faster semantic layers still assumes an agent is just a very fast analyst on a central plane. His working prototype gives each agent its own embedded engine using DuckDB, DuckLake, Quack, and Malloy, letting agents exchange data products directly. The open question he surfaces: what contracts govern analytical exchange once no single system owns the semantic and computational authority.

title: Why A Frontier Data Agent Outperforms General Coding Agents in Quality and Cost
blurb: Databricks benchmarked its Genie Code data agent against three general-purpose coding agents on 401 real internal tasks, each capped at a 20-minute budget, and found Genie Code was both the most accurate and the most cost efficient. The argument is that data agents can't lean on the verifiable tests coding agents use, so semantic catalog search, persistent memory of trusted tables, and enterprise context let Genie Code skip the token-burning random-walk exploration that sinks general agents.

title: DuckDB Internals: Why is DuckDB Fast? (Part 2 Vectorized Execution)
blurb: DuckDB's second internals post explains vectorized execution: instead of processing one row at a time, DuckDB pushes batches of values through each operator, keeping the CPU fed rather than stalling on memory waits and instruction dependencies. The payoff is concrete. The 2005 MonetDB/X100 benchmarks it cites clocked MySQL's row-at-a-time engine at 0.8 IPC and ~49 cycles per addition, versus ~2.2 cycles for the batched approach that DuckDB now builds on.

title: Aurora DSQL: Scalable, Multi-Region OLTP
blurb: Amazon's engineers detail Aurora DSQL, a distributed PostgreSQL-compatible OLTP database that disaggregates compute, transaction commit, and storage into independently scalable layers with active-active multi-region writes. The design leans on optimistic concurrency control and precise EC2 clock synchronization to guarantee strong consistency without a two-phase commit coordinator on the hot path. The bet is that decoupling the write path from storage lets a single database scale horizontally while surviving regional failure, a trade-off that swaps lock-based contention for commit-time conflict aborts.

title: Databricks and Microsoft expand Azure partnership into the 2030s
blurb: Databricks and Microsoft extended their strategic partnership into the 2030s, with Databricks moving core operations and its unified lakehouse onto Azure and expanding use of Azure Cobalt Arm chips, adopting Cobalt 200 for data-intensive and agentic AI workloads. In return, Microsoft will deepen Databricks integrations across Microsoft 365, Teams, Copilot, Power BI, OneLake, Purview and Foundry, including Genie and Unity AI Gateway for governing agents, models and costs.

title: The startup's Postgres survival guide
blurb: The author distills two years of running Postgres in production at Hatchet into a survival guide for startup engineers, organized from schema design and query basics through the query planner, bulk updates, and autovacuum tuning. The core argument is that schemas are the hardest thing to change after deployment, so teams should design them iteratively around actual read/write patterns rather than chasing textbook normal forms. It also flags where ORMs become a ceiling, since many scaling optimizations require dropping to raw SQL through tools like sqlc or Prisma TypedSQL.

title: If You Think You Can Do Real-World Text-to-SQL
blurb: The author argues that benchmark scores like those on Spider and BIRD wildly overstate how well text-to-SQL works against real production databases, where schemas are sprawling and undocumented, column names are cryptic, and business questions are ambiguous. The post walks through why enterprise schemas with hundreds of joined tables, inconsistent data, and implicit domain rules break models that ace academic benchmarks. The takeaway for data teams is that the hard part is schema semantics and context, not SQL generation itself.

title: Connect Amazon S3 data to Databricks with Delegated IAM Permissions
blurb: Databricks added a new external location creation flow that connects Amazon S3 to Unity Catalog using temporary AWS IAM delegation, replacing the old process of hand-authoring 140-line trust policies, setting bucket permissions, and deploying CloudFormation across two consoles. After you specify a bucket and access level, Databricks provisions least-privilege IAM roles on your behalf in a single workspace session, and users without sufficient AWS permissions can request them from their admin inline.

title: Introducing AI spend controls with Unity AI Gateway
blurb: Databricks announced AI Spend Controls in Unity AI Gateway, letting teams set budget alerts and hard spend caps at the user, workspace, use case, or account level, with requests automatically blocked once a limit is hit. It extends the gateway's existing cost visibility to cover foundation model usage across coding agents, production agents, and batch jobs, and ties into Unity Catalog system tables and Databricks budgets for unified AI cost governance.

title: Benchmarking the Full Pipeline of Materialized-View-Based Query Rewriting
blurb: This paper benchmarks materialized-view query acceleration as a full pipeline: candidate enumeration, view selection under storage budgets, and optimizer rewriting, rather than studying one stage in isolation. Using a modular framework with controlled ablations and a cross-engine protocol, the authors find strong interaction effects across stages and wide variability in MV usage and realized savings across academic methods and open-source and commercial systems. They also catalog recurring failure modes that explain regressions after rewriting.

title: Simplify AI agent orchestration with Lakebase Postgres
blurb: CLA and Databricks built a Databricks-native orchestration layer for long-running agentic document processing, using Lakebase Postgres to handle task queueing, retries, cost attribution, and real-time observability without separate infrastructure. Lakebase separates storage from compute, letting compute scale with demand while storage stays durable, which cuts document extraction time from hours to minutes across contracts, invoices, and financial filings.

title: How Our Universal Content Processing Platform Riviera Evolved for AI and Beyond
blurb: Dropbox opened Riviera, the content-processing platform that has powered its file previews for roughly a decade, to outside developers via API and MCP tools. What began as an internal preview service now handles transcoding, PDF flattening, and text extraction across Search, Replay, Sign, and Dash, producing the equivalent of 8 years of video daily. The write-up covers the architectural decisions that let a single preview service become shared infrastructure, and why AI document pipelines drove the external release.

title: Auto-Fill: Learning to Predict Missing Values Accurately with Specialist Language Models
blurb: This paper introduces Auto-Fill, an approach to predicting missing cell values in tabular data that post-trains three specialist small language models, each optimized for one capability: world knowledge, text-based reasoning, or code-based reasoning. A calibrated ensemble picks the most confident specialist or abstains, cutting the hallucinated predictions that make frontier reasoning models unreliable for data cleaning. Across 11 benchmarks and 2,200 real tables, it beat o3-pro, Gemini 3 Pro, and DeepSeek R1 on accuracy at under 1% of their cost.

title: Worst-Case Optimal BGPs on Temporal Graphs
blurb: This paper presents an index that evaluates basic graph patterns over temporal graphs, where each edge carries an interval of validity, in worst-case-optimal time using O(N) space for N edges. The authors extend Leapfrog Triejoin to handle time-aware quads (subject, property, object, time) under any variable ordering, and get the same guarantees for snapshot, version, and other temporal query variants. On real datasets, realistic queries return in milliseconds with low space overhead.

title: Hollywood: Towards a Large Movie Dataset for Database Benchmarking
blurb: This paper introduces Hollywood, a synthetic IMDb-compatible benchmark generator that pairs LLM-generated semantic dictionaries with deterministic temporal-graph data generation, unlike the fixed JOB/IMDb dump it replaces. The preliminary Hollywood-200K release ships 19.7M rows and 213 JOB-Light, JOB, and JOB-Complex queries, and induces cardinality estimation errors matching or exceeding the original IMDb dataset on two open systems.

title: Demonstrating GenDB: Instance-Optimized and Customized Query Processing Code Generation via LLM Agents
blurb: GenDB is a generative query engine that uses LLM agents to write instance-optimized query execution code tailored to specific data, workloads, and hardware, rather than relying on a hand-built engine. It targets repetitive, templated queries where upfront generation cost amortizes across runs, pairing with a traditional DBMS for ad-hoc queries. The prototype beats state-of-the-art engines on TPC-H and a leakage-resistant benchmark, with correctness enforced through fuzz testing.

title: From maintenance to innovation: Checkout's migration to Managed Service for Apache Airflow
blurb: Checkout.com migrated its data orchestration from self-hosted Apache Airflow on another cloud to Google Cloud's Managed Service for Apache Airflow (Cloud Composer 3), offloading patching, scaling, and dependency management to cut both maintenance overhead and peak-capacity costs. The old setup provisioned workers for peak load around the clock and took six minutes to sync DAGs after deployment, so the switch to dynamic scaling targets the recurring "Day 2" tax of running your own orchestrator.

title: RCC: Speculative Write Versioning with Redo Logs
blurb: This paper introduces RCC, a concurrency control scheme that uses redo logs to let write-write conflicting transactions run speculatively instead of serializing behind each other. Each transaction creates out-of-place speculative versions and installs them only at commit, with commit-time deadlock detection and column-granule logging to avoid false conflicts. On MySQL and PostgreSQL, RCC improves TPC-C throughput and latency by an order of magnitude at 64 threads, and scales to 128 clients on high-contention YCSB where competing schemes stall past 32.

title: Smart caching with CTE on Spark
blurb: Caching in Spark speeds up repeated computations by holding intermediate results in memory, but this piece argues the win only materializes for medium-sized, frequently reused datasets. Tiny tables gain nothing, and tables too large for memory trigger disk spills or eviction that slow the job down. The practical guidance for CTEs and nested queries: cache only when reuse is guaranteed, and watch the Spark UI storage tab to confirm the cache is actually paying off.

title: AI Schema Analyzer: Powered by Iceberg and Lakekeeper
blurb: The post walks through building an AI schema-drift analyzer for Iceberg tables, wiring Lakekeeper's REST catalog to emit change events to Kafka the moment a DDL runs, then feeding those events to a FastAPI service that calls the Anthropic SDK for impact analysis. Because Lakekeeper pushes catalog changes directly to Kafka with no metadata scanning, the agent flags breaking downstream dependencies within seconds and posts recommendations to Slack.

title: Backfilling: the most underrated feature of a data pipeline
blurb: Backfilling gets treated as a throwaway migration script written under pressure, then abandoned in a scripts/ folder to rot. The author argues both forms of backfill, historical ingestion and system-to-system migration, are the same problem and belong inside the production extract-raw-transform pipeline rather than one-off jobs. Running historical loads through the same tested stages as tomorrow's data cuts the time teams spend fighting their own pipeline.

title: Provisioning for the Agentic Era: How Databricks Built a Self-Serve Infrastructure Vending Machine
blurb: Databricks' field engineering team outgrew its shared-workspace model and built FEVM, a self-serve system that provisions governed, Databricks-native environments on demand and tears them down when the work is done. The write-up details why workspaces optimized for a few admins managing many users broke down for an org where nearly everyone needs admin access, and how contention, cost attribution, and observability degraded as GTM headcount passed 7,000. Agents are treated as first-class users of the provisioning flow.

title: DMG: A Scalable and Efficient Memory-Disaggregated Graph Processing System
blurb: DMG is the first graph processing system on disaggregated memory that scales past a single compute and memory node, sidestepping the oversized compute-side caches prior DM designs required. It pairs a DM-friendly graph store with an adaptive update coordinator and a two-stage workload manager for partitioning and runtime rescheduling. Against the prior state-of-the-art, the authors report up to 4.9X better performance while cutting compute-side cache demand by up to 18.9X.

title: Flux 3
blurb: Black Forest Labs released FLUX 3 in early access, a multimodal foundation model that jointly trains on images, video, and audio to generate stills or synced video-plus-audio from text or image and video references. Built on the lab's Self-Flow method for aligning generation and understanding in one architecture, it targets both content creation and physical AI, though early-access limits mean production workflows should wait.

title: A glimpse behind the scenes of Coca-Cola's new branding
blurb: Jones Knowles Ritchie released a behind-the-scenes video on Coca-Cola's identity refresh, built on a brief to "make Coca-Cola more Coca-Cola" by amplifying its core brand assets and unifying the system across territories and touchpoints. The film leans on retro imagery to show how JKR sharpened the existing equities rather than reinventing them, a restraint-first approach worth studying for anyone rationalizing a sprawling global brand.

title: Autodesk’s SIGGRAPH announcements look unrelated, but they all solve the same huge problem
blurb: Autodesk used SIGGRAPH 2026 to ship updates across Maya, 3ds Max, Flow Production Tracking, and Arnold, including a MotionMaker feature that lets studios train custom generative motion models on their own rigs and mocap. The scattered-looking announcements share one goal: compressing the gap between idea and rendered result by cutting the waiting built into 3D pipelines, from render queues to remote review to rebuilding real-world assets Gaussian Splats could capture.

title: The screens are getting demoted
blurb: The author argues that much of UI exists only to bridge the gap between what a user wants and what the software forces them to do by hand, and that AI is collapsing that gap by acting on intent directly. As natural-language commands replace multi-step flows like compose-attach-browse-select-send, the piece contends that many screens are becoming fallbacks rather than the primary interface designers build around.

title: Four questions every tool should answer (even Figma) about AI training model usage
blurb: The author argues that Figma's November 2025 class-action suit is less about copyright than about defaults, framing the opt-in-versus-opt-out choice for AI training on customer files as a design decision teams make on their users' behalf. The piece offers a four-question, twenty-minute trust test for auditing your own product's training defaults, using Zoom's and Slack's quiet opt-out enrollments as cautionary precedent.

title: Is the Tinder rebrand really a success? Not everyone is so sure...
blurb: Jade Horton, Strategy Partner at Elmwood Brand Consultants, argues Tinder's decade-first rebrand nails the visuals but not the substance: the bold flame, more confident type, and shift from soft pink to grown-up red set an ambition the product doesn't yet deliver. She points to the gap between the identity's warm talk of honesty and a feature that lets AI scan your camera roll to rate chemistry, a mismatch already trending as "creepy" on TikTok and Reddit.

title: Derek&Eric embraces the chaos with a joyfully bold brand for The Glorious Mess
blurb: Derek&Eric, an independent London studio, created The Glorious Mess, a new dessert brand for meringue maker Flower & White built around the happy-accident origin of the Eton Mess. The identity leans into that chaos with expressive typography, juicy fruit illustrations, and energetic photography, giving a category thin on distinctive branding a flexible, unmistakably British system that scales across new flavors and formats.

title: Koto helps restaurant-discovery app Franki make time dance
blurb: Koto gave Franki, the merchant-funded restaurant-discovery app, a warm yellow-soaked identity built on the line "make time dance," positioning it against Google, Tripadvisor, Yelp, and a wave of video-first discovery rivals. The concept treats time as the currency of dining out, translating into an energetic, motion-led system meant to sell an app-only experience and keep users posting recommendations after download.

title: Manica unveils Kansas City Chiefs stadium topped with transparent dome
blurb: Manica released its design for a 70,000-seat, fully enclosed Kansas City Chiefs stadium topped with a transparent dome, replacing Arrowhead Stadium at a cost of $3 billion. The venue is set to open in 2031, and the clear roof lets the team run a climate-controlled interior while preserving the daylit, open-air feel of the original.

title: Glasgow School of Art "cannot rebuild" Mackintosh Building
blurb: The Glasgow School of Art said it cannot afford to rebuild Charles Rennie Mackintosh's 1910 building without external funding, eight years after the 2018 fire gutted the A-listed structure for the second time. The admission leaves the future of one of Britain's most significant Art Nouveau landmarks dependent on a fundraising effort the school has not yet scoped, with no reinstatement timeline attached.

title: Algae "biocells" powered by photosynthesis could replace disposable batteries
blurb: Bio-designer Lucia Giron, working with the University of Cambridge's Department of Biochemistry, built prototypes of algae-based "biocells" that generate electricity through photosynthesis as a replacement for disposable batteries. The design leans on Cambridge's biophotovoltaics research, where living algae act like biological solar panels converting light into current, pointing toward low-toxicity power sources for the small devices that currently drain single-use cells.

title: Flint: A Semantics-Driven Data Visualization Intermediate Language
blurb: Flint is an intermediate language for data visualization that lets authors write concise, semantics-driven specs, declaring what data fields mean structurally instead of hand-configuring scales, axes, and formatting. A hierarchical semantic model lets the compiler derive and optimize library-agnostic configs, then compile them to Vega-Lite, Apache ECharts, and other target grammars for both human and AI authoring.

title: CRAFT: Exploring Wearable Creative AI on Smart Glasses for Fiction Writing in Real-World Contexts
blurb: CRAFT is a research prototype for AI smart glasses that turns a writer's real-world surroundings into fiction prompts, translating in-situ experiences like serendipitous street encounters into narrative ideas. Across interviews, co-design workshops, and 24 field-trial sessions with writers, the authors surface concrete design goals for wearable creative AI: augment perception without breaking immersion, ground authenticity in lived experience, and preserve creative agency and the boundary between life and art.

title: A Framework of User Experience Principles for Human-AI Agent Interaction in the Workplace
blurb: This paper proposes eight core UX principles for human-AI agent interaction in enterprise workflows, validated through participatory design workshops, expert review, meta-analysis, and interviews. The principles function as actionable guardrails for the trust and adoption problems designers now face as agents move into daily business tools, offering a structured starting point before empirical norms exist.

title: Building Ridgeline: Engineering a Real-Time 3D Experience in Webflow
blurb: A developer documents building Ridgeline, a Webflow site that renders three real alpine treks as live Three.js terrain from actual elevation data (SRTM DEMs and cleaned GPX tracks), rather than video loops or baked sprites, while keeping the project human-editable. The build log covers the core architecture decision of getting real-time 3D into Webflow, and details a stack of React Three Fiber, GSAP, and Blender assembled in about a week through the Webflow MCP.

title: Steal this UX framework from Dubai’s most over-the-top hotel
blurb: A Google UX researcher argues that hospitality design, not app design, is the richer source of interaction patterns, using a week at Atlantis The Palm to extract reusable UX principles from things like a chocolate fountain that never spills. The framing turns invisible service choreography into design heuristics, a useful prompt to study systems that manage human behavior in physical space rather than reverse-engineering competitor screens.

title: I learned 5 fundamental truths of game art direction, which aren't always about art
blurb: An art director's talk at Develop: Brighton argues that game art direction starts from first principles rather than aesthetics: define a single creative North Star, then let asset density follow the player's micro, macro, and meta loops. The Skyrim-versus-Arc Raiders comparison is the sharpest takeaway, showing how identical walking-speed density forces very different art budgets depending on map scale.

title: Kenta Hirayama clads House in Kinuta in steel-sheet facade
blurb: Kenta Hirayama's House in Kinuta in Tokyo pairs a steel-sheet facade with a simple gabled roof to maximize light and privacy.
